steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers caution against concluding something. The key detail is that supplementation data was "rare, highly varied, and poorly tracked" — so any conclusion drawn from that data is unreliable.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — vitamin D levels among zoo chimpanzees in Italy and other mid-latitude countries likely show different seasonal patterns than do vitamin D levels among wild chimpanzees in Senegal and other tropical countries.: This is about seasonal patterns, not supplementation. The caution is specifically about what the flawed supplementation data can prove. ✗
  • Choice B — the lack of an observed effect of supplemental vitamin D on zoo chimpanzees in Italy and other mid-latitude countries may reflect shortcomings in the data rather than actual ineffectiveness.: This is actually what the researchers ARE saying — they acknowledge the data is flawed and that the null result may not be real. This is their position, not what they caution against. ✗
  • Choice C — providing zoo chimpanzees in Italy and other mid-latitude countries with supplemental vitamin D has no promise as a strategy for offsetting winter reductions in their vitamin D levels.: This is a definitive negative conclusion drawn directly from the flawed supplementation data. The researchers caution against exactly this: declaring supplementation worthless based on unreliable data. ✓
  • Choice D — winter reductions in UVB irradiance in Italy and other mid-latitude countries can contribute to variations in zoo chimpanzees' vitamin D levels.: This conclusion comes from the seasonal UVB data, which was well-documented — not from the supplementation data that was poorly tracked. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
